coredata.dc:title: A biocompatible electrochemical sensor based on PtNi alloy nanoparticles-coupled N-GQDs for in situ monitoring of dopamine in glioma cells
coredata.dc:creator: Panda, A.K.
Murugan, K.
Sakthivel, R.
Dhawan, U.
Lin, L.-Y.
Duann, Y.-F.
He, J.-H.
Chung, R.-J.
coredata.dc:description: We report the design and construction of enzyme-free sensor using platinum–nickel (PtNi) bimetallic alloy nanoparticle-conjugated nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) for the highly specific in situ monitoring of dopamine (DA) secreted by glioma cells (C6). PtNi@N-GQDs nanocomposites were synthesized using a simple ultrasonication method. The resulting hybrid material was an excellent electrocatalyst for the redox activity of DA owing to the combined properties of PtNi alloys and highly conductive N-GQDs. The PtNi@N-GQDs-based sensing platform demonstrated substantial sensing ability with a detection range of 0.0125–952 μM, a sensitivity of 0.279 μA/μM/cm2, and a limit of detection of 0.005 μM (S/N = 3). The sensing performance of PtNi@N-GQDs was highly stable, selective, and reproducible. We successfully showed the practical application of the PtNi@N-GQDs sensor by quantifying DA in the blood serum and human urine samples. Finally, we used the PtNi@N-GQDs biocompatible platform to quantify DA released from C6 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Neurotransmitter
Dopamine
N-doped graphene quantum dots
Glioma cells
Enzyme-free sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of DA in C6 cells
Electrochemical detection of DA at PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE
Amperometric determination of DA at PtNi@N-GQDs
Repeatability, reproducibility, and stability of the PtNi@N-GQDs sensor
Detection of DA released by live cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation and fabrication of PtNi@N-GQDs composite based electode towards detection of dopamine in C6 cells.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of (a) PtNi, (b) N-GQDs, and (c) PtNi@N-GQDs. Field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) image of (d) PtNi@N-GQDs, (e) energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) spectrum of PtNi@N-GQDs, and (f) FESEM-EDX mapping images of PtNi@N-GQDs nanocomposite.
(a) X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of N-GQDs, PtNi, and PtNi@N-GQDs, (b) Raman spectra of N-GQDs, and PtNi@N-GQDs.
(a) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey of PtNi@N-GQDs; magnified spectra of (b) Pt 4f, (c) Ni 2p, (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) N 1s.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry (CV) responses of bare screen-printed electrode (SPE), PtNi/SPE, N-GQDs/SPE, and PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE for 100 μM dopamine (DA) in 0.05 M PB at 0.05 V/s, (b) the bar diagram for Ip of DA versus different modified SPEs, (c) CVs of PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE for successive addition of DA (0–400 μM), (d) linear plot of Ip and concentration of DA (μM), (e) CVs of PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE for 100 μM DA at scan rates from 0.02 V/s to 0.3 V/s, (f) linear plot for the Ip of DA and scan rates (V/s).

(a) Cyclic voltammetry of PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE for 100 μM dopamine (DA) at various pH (5.5–7.5), (b) plot for the Ip of DA and pH, (c) cyclic stability of PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE, and (d) long-term stability of PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE over 25 days.

(a) Amperometric (i–t) behavior of dopamine (DA) with increasing concentration (0–687 μM) at PtNi@N-GQDs under various working potentials (0.01, 0.02, 0.03, and 0.04 V), (b) linear plot of Ipa versus [DA] for various Epa.

(a) Amperometric (i–t) response of PtNi@N-GQDs with consecutive addition of dopamine (DA) (0.0125–952 μM) at 0.04 V, (b) calibration plot of Ipa versus DA concentrations, (c) response time for steady-state current, (d) effects of interfering species such as H2O2, caffeic acid (CA), glucose (GLU), urea, L-cysteine (L-cys), sucrose (SUC), ascorbic acid (AA), and hydroquinone (HQ) on the responses of PtNi@N-GQDs for DA in PB.

(a) Repeatability of PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE at five independent measurements for 100 μM dopamine in PB; (b) bar diagram of obtained redox current (Ip) and five different measurements; (c) cyclic voltammetry for reproducibility test at PtNi@N-GQDs sensor, and (d) bar diagram of Ip and five different PtNi@N-GQDs/SPEs.
The real sample detection of dopamine [DA] at PtNi@N-GQDs in (a) blood serum and (c) human urine in 0.05 M PB. Ipa versus [DA] for (b) blood serum and (d) human urine samples.
(a) Viability of C6 cells and microscopic images of (b) C6 cells.

Amperometric result of PtNi@N-GQDs to the addition of K+ ions (0, 10, 15 μM) in PB at 0.04 V (a) without C6 cells and with C6 cells; (b) corresponding current responses for C6 cells with varying concentrations of K+ ions.
The electrochemical oxidation mechanism of dopamine at PtNi@N-GQDs nanocomposites.
Comparison of the different parameters of the PtNi@N-GQDs sensor with reported literature.
Detection of DA in human blood serum and human urine samples at PtNi@N-GQDs electrode.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation (RSD) of three independent experiments (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A biocompatible electrochemical sensor based on PtNi alloy nanoparticles-coupled N-GQDs for in situ monitoring of dopamine in glioma cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, National Taipei University of Technology (Taipei Tech), Taipei 10608, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology
National Taipei University of Technology (Taipei Tech)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ethiraj College for Women, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for the Cellular Microenvironment, Division of Biomedical Engineering, School of Engineering, University of Glasgow, Glasgow, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for the Cellular Microenvironment, Division of Biomedical Engineering, School of Engineering, University of Glasgow, Glasgow, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, City University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, City University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the design and construction of enzyme-free sensor using platinum–nickel (PtNi) bimetallic alloy nanoparticle-conjugated nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) for the highly specific in situ monitoring of dopamine (DA) secreted by glioma cells (C6). PtNi@N-GQDs nanocomposites were synthesized using a simple ultrasonication method. The resulting hybrid material was an excellent electrocatalyst for the redox activity of DA owing to the combined properties of PtNi alloys and highly conductive N-GQDs. The PtNi@N-GQDs-based sensing platform demonstrated substantial sensing ability with a detection range of 0.0125–952 μM, a sensitivity of 0.279 μA/μM/cm2, and a limit of detection of 0.005 μM (S/N = 3). The sensing performance of PtNi@N-GQDs was highly stable, selective, and reproducible. We successfully showed the practical application of the PtNi@N-GQDs sensor by quantifying DA in the blood serum and human urine samples. Finally, we used the PtNi@N-GQDs biocompatible platform to quantify DA released from C6 cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is an essential monoamine neurotransmitter in humans and mammals involved in various physiological functions, including learning, motivation, arousal, reward signaling, and motor control [1]. Abnormal DA levels are associated with severe neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's, neuromuscular disorders, schizophrenia, heart failure, and hyperactive disorders [2]. The DA in the cytoplasm rapidly forms reactive oxygen species, leading to increased oxidative stress and neurodegeneration [3]. Fluctuations in cytoplasmic DA levels might impact other metabolites and the functionality of DA, which could provide more information about neurodegenerative diseases [4]. Hence, it is important to design highly effective and sensitive sensors for the accurate determination of DA release from living cells [5]. Various techniques, including field-effect transistors, electrochemiluminescence, and electrochemical (EC) methods have been extensively explored as DA sensors owing to their portable sensing platforms with rapid detection rates [6–8]. Among them, EC methods have been widely studied because of the presence of electroactive DA molecules that can be readily analyzed without any enzymes, ligands, or reporter probes [9]. However, the EC approaches have numerous restrictions, particularly the selectivity and sensitivity of DA detection in the presence of other redox-active biomolecules such as uric acid, ascorbic acid, glucose, and other catecholamine derivatives [10]. There are two types of sensors for the EC detection of biomolecules such as enzyme-based and enzyme-free, respectively [11]. The enzyme-based sensing platforms have been employed by immobilizing enzymes on the electrode surfaces. However, these enzyme-based sensors are having good sensitivity, but their disadvantages such as high cost, the mobility of enzymes, instability in pH variable, and short life time are obstacles the economical acceptability [12]. On the other hand, the enzyme-free sensors are rapid, sensitive, and effective to provide great sensing platform because of easy operation, cost-effective, and high stability. Based on this consideration, the enzyme-free EC sensors are appropriate for DA detection [10].

Recently, alloy nanoparticles (NPs) have attracted considerable interest in various fields. Platinum (Pt) metal is widely used as an electrocatalyst owing to its superior catalytic properties [13]. However, Pt NPs tend to self-aggregate, which hinders their catalytic activity [14]. Therefore, the addition of another metal along with Pt can produce Pt-based alloys (M = Au, Ag, Pd, Ni, and Ru), thus tuning their electronic arrangement, leading to enhanced EC performance [15]. Nickel (Ni) is widely used in batteries, electroplating, and other applications because of its good conductivity, excellent temperature resistance, and high corrosion resistance [16]. PtNi bimetallic alloys exhibit superior catalytic activities compared to single metals, and the alloying strategy can also decrease the cost of the material [17]. Various studies have been proposed the incorporation of PtNi alloys with a highly conductive carbon matrix, including graphene, graphene aerogels, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon cloth, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), to enhance their conductivity and produce more active sites [17,18]. Among the various carbon materials, N-GQDs have the potential to develop heterostructures owing to their exceptional electronic properties and large specific surface area [19]. By developing a PtNi@N-GQDs heterostructure, the aggregation problem in the composite material can be resolved, and the number of active heterointerfaces improved [20]. There are two types of nitrogen bonding configurations within the carbon network: (i) oxidized form (N atoms bonded to O atoms) and (ii) lattice N atoms, namely, pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and graphitic N [21]. These N functionalities of N-GQDs could increase the number of active sites catalytically and are beneficial for the detection of DA at low concentrations. In this study, we designed an enzyme-free EC sensor using PtNi and N-GQDs. The combination of the PtNi nanostructure with highly conductive N-GQDs provides an attractive platform for improving the EC performance of cellular DA detection.

Nickel (II) nitrate hexahydrate (Ni (NO3)2.6H2O, ≥ 99.9%), platinum (II) acetylacetonate (Pt (acac)2, ≥97%), dimethylformamide (DMF), polyvinylpyrrolidone ((C6H9NO)n), ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, ≥99.1%), citric acid (C₆H₈O₇, ≥99.5%), diethylenetriamine (HN(CH₂CH₂NH₂)₂, ≥99.0%), dopamine hydrochloride (DA, ≥ 98.5%), and other required chemicals were bought from Sigma-Aldrich, Taiwan. For the preparation of phosphate buffer (PB, 0.05 M), a mixture of sodium phosphate monobasic and dibasic NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 was used. The pH of the PB was adjusted using NaOH (≥98%) and HCl (36.5–38%). Human serum was collected from a healthy volunteer at Chang-Gung memorial hospital, and this experimental protocol was approved by IRB No.201801660B. Double-distilled (DD) water was used for synthesis and other washing purposes. The screen-printed electrodes (SPEs) were acquired from Zensor (USA) (model TE-100).

Morphological characterization was investigated with the help of a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (TEM/JEM 2100 F; JEOL Ltd., Japan) and a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM/JEOL JSM- 7100 F). The elemental percentages and compositions were observed using energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Crystallinity and structural defects in carbon materials were detected via X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, X'Pert3 Powder, PANalytical, the Netherlands) and Raman spectroscopy (Ramboss 500i Micro (DINGWOO/USA). EC studies were carried out in the CH Instruments 6041E workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China). In cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis, a single SPE was employed as the reference, working, and counter electrodes. Rotating disc electrode with a rotating speed of 1200 rpm, Pt wire, and Ag/AgCl are acted as working, auxiliary, and reference electrodes, respectively, for the amperometry studies, and the amperometry experiments were done at a potential of 0.04 V.

PtNi alloy was synthesized via a solvothermal approach using DMF according to our previous literature [22]. First, 100 mg of Pt (acac)2 and 100 mg of Ni (NO3)2.6H2O were dissolved in DMF (80 mL) under magnetic stirring. Subsequently, ascorbic acid (0.5 g) and 0.8 g polyvinylpyrrolidon were supplemented to the homogeneous solution as the reducing agent and surfactant were mixed properly with stirring conditions for 30 min. Then, the resulting mixture was subjected to solvothermal reaction at 180 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the mixture in the stainless steel was cooled, collected, and washed five times with hexane and ethanol under centrifugation at 6000 rpm. Finally, it was dried at 80 °C for 12h.

The N-GQDs were obtained under hydrothermal conditions according to a previously reported procedure [23]. Citric acid (1 mM) and diethylenetriamine (3 mM) were added to DD water (50 mL) and stirred in the solution until form a uniform mixture. Subsequently, the mixture was subjected to a hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 6 h. Once the mixture in the stainless steel was cooled, the product was washed by centrifugation using ethanol. The material was fully dried overnight at 60 °C to attain N-GQDs.

Ultrasonication was used to synthesize the composite (Fig. 1 ). First, a 1:1 ratio of PtNi and N-GQDs were taken in the beaker containing DD water and stirred well for 30 min. Subsequently, the material was centrifuged to remove water and dried. The final product is denoted as PtNi@N-GQDs.

A sample of 1 mg/mL, synthesized PtNi@N-GQDs NPs was dispersed in DD water under ultrasonication for 30 min to obtain homogeneous slurry. PtNi@N-GQDs (∼7.0 μL) suspension was drop cast on the surface of SPE and exposed to dry in a 50 °C oven for 40 min to acquire PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE. Bare SPE, PtNi/SPE, and N-GQDs/SPE were prepared for control studies.

Rat glioma (C6, ATCC, USA) cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplied with penicillin (100 U/mL), streptomycin (100 μg/mL), fetal bovine serum (FBS,10%), and C6 cells were seeded using T75 flasks in a CO2 incubator kept at 37 °C and 95% humidity.

The biocompatibility of PtNi@N-GQDs was analyzed through MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay. We prepared 5 mg/mL of MTT reagent and kept it at 4 °C. To assess in vitro cytotoxicity of PtNi@N-GQDs, the stock solution was diluted 10 times with the DMEM culture medium. C6 cells (1000 per well) were cultured using 96-well plates for 24 h. The cells were washed repeatedly using phosphate buffered saline (PBS). Then, 0.5 mL of MTT reagent in DMEM was added to the cells, and the plates were incubated for 4 h in the dark. The MTT reagent was eliminated, and formazan crystals were dissolved in DMSO (200 μL/well). The 96-well plates were then gently shaken for 10 min at RT. Finally, the absorbance was measured microplate reader at 570 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in C6 cells
ce:section.ce:para: C6 cells were cultured in DMEM until they reached confluence. The culture medium was removed after a 90% confluence was attained. Afterward, the cells were washed twice using PBS and counted using a hemocytometer. Then, the cells (1 × 107) were incubated with two diverse concentrations of K+ ions (5 and 10 μM) and dispersed in PB (80 mL, pH 7.0). Amperometry experiments were conducted in the cells at a potential of 0.04 V. PB with K+ ion and without cells was used as the blank.

The morphological features of PtNi, N-GQDs, and PtNi@N-GQDs were studied using TEM and FESEM (Fig. 2 ). TEM observation of PtNi is shown in Fig. 2a which confirms that the average size of synthesized PtNi alloy NPs are around 5–6 nm. TEM image of the N-GQDs in Fig. 2b shows nanodots with an average size of 0.5–1 nm, smaller than that of PtNi NPs. The TEM image of the PtNi@N-GQDs is displayed in Fig. 2c. The FESEM image of PtNi@N-GQDs in Fig. 2d displays that aggregation of both N-GQDs and PtNi was observed during composite formation, which may be a typical phenomenon for all NPs. The EDX spectrum of PtNi@N-GQDs (Fig. 2e) shows the Pt, Ni, carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) with weight percentages of 31.3, 21.7, 25.5, 12.03, and 9.02%, respectively. EDX mapping of PtNi@N-GQDs (Fig. 2f) confirmed that Pt, Ni, C, N, and O were evenly distributed in the composite, further confirming the formation of the nanocomposite.

XRD was exploited to characterize the crystalline structures of N-GQDs, PtNi, and PtNi@N-GQDs (Fig. 3 a). As shown in Fig. 3a, a typical peak for the crystalline graphite-like structure of N-GQDs was observed at a 2θ value of 26.8°, which is consistent with the (002) plane (JCPDS Card No.: 41–1487) [24]. The peaks of PtNi at 2θ angles of 40.1°, 48.2°, 68.3°, and 83.6° agree with the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes, respectively, representing the fcc structure (JCPDS 65–9445) specifying the alloy formation without other impurities like oxides contain Pt and Ni [25]. The XRD pattern of the PtNi@N-GQDs exhibited characteristic peaks of both N-GQDs and PtNi with high crystallinity. Moreover, the peak intensity of (002) plane in the PtNi@N-GQDs was almost decreased compared to the pristine N-GQD. This reduction of peak intensity is due to distorted crystalline structure attributed by the effective interaction of PtNi NPs with N-GQDs. Moreover, the peak at the 2θ of about 15° may be arising due to the formation of the polycrystalline structure during composite formation.

The structural defects in the prepared materials were inspected using Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 3b). Two intense characteristic peaks of the carbon materials, D (1349 cm−1) and G (1564 cm−1) bands, were observed for the N-GQDs and PtNi@N-GQDs, respectively. The D band indicates disorders in sp2 hybridized carbon, and G refers to the in-plane vibrations of sp2 carbon (C=C) [26]. In PtNi@N-GQDs, the intensity ratio between D and G bands (ID/IG) was higher (∼0.99) than that of N-GQDs (∼0.97), indicating that the formation of N-GQDs in the PtNi alloy network led to a disordered structure.

XPS was used to scrutinize the composition, chemical states, and bonding of the PtNi@N-GQDs composite. The survey spectrum of PtNi@N-GQDs composite indicated the occurrence of specific elements such as Pt, Ni, C, O, and N (Fig. 4 a). The magnified XPS scan of Pt 4f (Fig. 4b) shows the two peaks at 74.1 eV and 70.6 eV ascribed to Pt 4f5/2 and Pt 4f7/2 for the PtNi nanoalloy, corresponding to the metallic Pt (0) state. Fig. 4c displays the Ni 2p scan, the peak at 852.6 eV and 870.7 eV attributed to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, respectively, consistent with the metallic Ni (0) state [27]. The spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 4d) has predominant peaks at 288.4, 286.7, and 284.5 eV arising from the O=C–O, C–N, and C=C/C–C bonds, respectively. Three peaks were found in the O 1s spectrum at 532.91, 532.64, and 530.71 eV belonging to the C-OH, C-O-N/C-O, and C=O bonds, respectively (Fig. 4e). The three peaks were fitted to the N 1s spectrum at 401.78, 400.5, and 399.4 eV, which agreed with the graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, and pyridine-N [28](Fig. 4f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of DA at PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE
ce:section.ce:para: The electrokinetic characteristics of PtNi@N-GQDs toward 100 μM DA were examined using CV at increasing scan rates (Fig. 5e). When scan rates increased from 0.02 to 0.3 mV/s, Ip of DA also increased. The shifting also happened in the redox peak potential (Ep). Linearity was noted between the Ip of DA against scan rates, and the regression and coefficient of Ipa were as follows: Ipa (μA) = 32.96 (V/s) + 0.700 and R2 = 0.996, respectively (Fig. 5f). Moreover, linearity was also obtained between Ipa of DA and the square root of scan rate (y = 23.089x - 2.8907 and R2 = 0.9904). These results indicate that the EC determination of DA on PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE was a partially surface-controlled and diffusion-controlled process.

The pH study was carried out for 100 μM DA in PB at various pH ranges (5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, and 7.5) at 0.05 V/s. Clear intensive redox CV curves were obtained for DA at all pH values (5.5–7.5). As shown in Fig. 6 a, when the pH increased from 5.5 to 7.5, Ep shifted toward negative direction. The current responses of PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE at various pH values are shown in Fig. 6b. In this study, DA exhibited a comparatively higher current at pH 7.0; therefore, pH 7.0 was considered to be suitable for further experiments.

The stability of the PtNi@N-GQDs electrode was evaluated using 50 repeated CV scans of 100 μM DA (Fig. 6c). In the 50th cycle, PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE retained 92.0% of its current response from its initial cycle toward DA, representing excellent EC stability of PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE. The long-term stability test for our proposed PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE was performed by CV over 25 days using 100 μM DA, and the results are presented as a bar diagram in Fig. 6d. The current response was recorded every 5 days interval using the same PtNi@N-GQDs electrode, which was kept in a 4 °C refrigerator. The PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE retained 91.0% of its initial value. This result proves that the PtNi@N-GQDs electrode has excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amperometric determination of DA at PtNi@N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Optimizing the appropriately applied potential is important for amperometric DA detection. The amperometric current versus time (i-t) plot was obtained at various potentials (0.01, 0.02, 0.03, and 0.04 V) with consecutive addition of DA (0–687 μM). The most stable and highest Ipa without noise was achieved at 0.04 V (Fig. 7 a). A linear curve was noted between Ipa and [DA] at various potentials (Fig. 7b). Therefore, 0.04 V was selected for amperometry-based DA detection.

The selectivity of the PtNi@N-GQDs toward the DA oxidation was investigated using several interferents. The i-t observation of DA in the presence of potential interferences in PtNi@N-GQDs was studied in PB (pH 7.0) at the optimized applied potential (Fig. 8d). The 100-fold excess concentrations of H2O2, caffeic acid, glucose, urea, L-cysteine, sucrose, ascorbic acid, and hydroquinone had no significant effect on the DA current response. In contrast, DA addition significantly enhanced the current response. The results indicate that the PtNi@N-GQDs have excellent selectivity for the determination of DA and are highly resistant to errors caused by interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability, reproducibility, and stability of the PtNi@N-GQDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The repeatability and reproducibility of PtNi@N-GQDs/SPE were explored using CV for 100 μM DA. For the repeatability test, five independent CV measurements were performed to detect DA (Fig. 9 a). The number of measurements and Ipa was plotted, and the obtained relative standard deviation was 4.24% (Fig. 9b). A sequence of five different PtNi@N-GQDs electrodes were used for the reproducibility test, and a CV experiment was conducted with 100 μM DA (Fig. 9c). As shown in the bar diagram (Fig. 9d), Ipa versus electrodes have exhibited minor deviations with 4.02% of relative standard deviation, which highlights the exceptional reproducibility of the PtNi@N-GQDs sensor.

The biocompatibility of PtNi@N-GQDs with the C6 cells was investigated before detection of DA in cells . We cultured C6 cells with various concentrations of PtNi@N-GQDs (6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 μg/mL), and the MTT assay was demonstrated. Cell viability decreased with increasing NPs concentrations. More than 94% of viability was observed for C6 cells at 100 μg/mL (Fig. 11 a), representing excellent biocompatibility of the PtNi@N-GQDs. Hence, PtNi@N-GQDs are suitable for biological analysis. A microscopic image of the C6 cells cultured with the PtNi@N-GQDs nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 11b. The cells were appeared to proliferate and healthy after 24 h of incubation with PtNi@N-GQDs (10 μg/mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA released by live cells
ce:section.ce:para: An amperometric experiment was carried out to determine DA release from C6 cells. K+ ions were used to stimulate the release of DA from live cells. C6 cells were cultured and incubated with different concentrations of K+ ion for 30 min, and the current was measured using amperometry at a potential of 0.04 V. The same experiment without C6 cells was performed as a blank. No current response was observed after the addition of K+ to PB without cells. Moreover, the DA oxidation current increased with an increase in the K+ ion concentration (0, 10, and 15 μM) incubated cells in PB (Fig. 12 a). The obtained current response of DA released from C6 cells is shown in Fig. 12b. When the K+ ion concentration was increased, the current of DA increased in the cells. This result reveals the PtNi@N-GQDs could be a favorable catalyst for the detection of DA levels in live cells.

Herein, we report a simple and unique approach for fabrication of PtNi@N-GQDs using a simple ultrasonication method. The TEM, FESEM, EDX, Raman, XRD, and XPS data provided evidence of the effective synthesis of the PtNi@N-GQDs composite. The PtNi@N-GQDs-modified SPE was exploited as a potential EC sensor for monitoring DA levels. Furthermore, the PtNi@N-GQDs sensor displayed superior analytical parameters such as a linear detection range (0.0125–952 μM) and LOD (0.005 μM at S/N = 3). The selectivity, stability, and reproducibility of the PtNi@N-GQDs sensor were also successfully demonstrated. The results of the long-term stability test showed that our sensor has retained 91% of its initial day response toward DA. These results can be attributed to the higher stability owing to the effective interaction between PtNi NPs and N-GQDs. The practical utility was successfully demonstrated at PtNi@N-GQDs sensors in real serum and urine samples with recoveries of 99.3% and 98.5%, respectively. Finally, we established that PtNi@N-GQDs could be a potential platform for in situ monitoring of DA in C6 cells. This study paves the way for to design of efficient non-enzymatic EC sensors for various biomolecules using a simple method.

Asit Kumar Panda: Writing – original draft, Writing – review and editing, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization. Keerthi Murugan: Writing – review and editing, Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration. Rajalakshmi Sakthivel: Writing – review and editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Udesh Dhawan: Conceptualization, Methodology. Lu-Yin Lin: Writing – review and editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Yeh-Fang Duann: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Jr-Hau He: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing – review and editing. Ren-Jei Chung: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support of this research from the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 106-2221-E-027-034; MOST 109-2222-E-027-004); and the International Distinguished Visiting Professor's support for Prof. Jr-Hau He from National Taipei University of Technology. Technical assistance from the Precision Analysis and Material Research Center of the National Taipei University of Technology (Taipei Tech) is appreciated.
